Home › Glossary › Single-Blind
Trial Design

Single-Blind — What It Means in Clinical Trials

Plain English Definition

In a single-blind trial, you do not know which treatment you are receiving, but the research team does. This setup is used when it is impractical to keep the doctors blinded — for example, when the treatments look very different or require different procedures.

Why It Matters

Understanding that you are blinded but your doctor is not can affect how you interpret your care during the trial. Your doctor still follows strict protocols to keep the study fair.

Example

You might see: "Single-blind study — participants will not know their treatment assignment." This means your doctor knows your group but will not reveal it to you.

Related Terms

  • Double-Blind — In a double-blind trial, neither you nor the doctors running the study know which treatment you are receiving.
  • Open-Label — In an open-label trial, everyone knows exactly which treatment each participant is receiving — both you and the research team.
  • Placebo — A placebo is a treatment that looks identical to the real drug but contains no active ingredient — sometimes called a "sugar pill.

Need help understanding eligibility criteria?
